data.title: Innovative Industrial: Why I Am Downgrading It
data.description:
I reiterated my Strong Buy on Innovative Industrial Properties, Inc. (IIPR) in December, and it is slightly lower now, but I am downgrading it to Hold. I continue to view it as a better option than many cannabis stocks, but I am concerned about the cannabis sector and REITs generally. In this piece, I explain why my views are less optimistic for IIPR.

Why I Like IIPR

As I stated in that last piece, IIPR seems very cheap. The stock trades below tangible book value (0.74X), and it has just a small amount of debt. The enterprise value to projected adjusted EBITDA for 2026 is just 8.9X, and the price to AFFO per share that I project ($7.19) is just 7X.

While I know that many are concerned about the move into partnership with IQHQ, I like that the company, which has employees that came from the life sciences REIT industry, has moved in this direction in order to diversify. When the management team entered the cannabis industry a decade ago, they did not know much about it. I credit them for doing a good job of learning! If 280E taxation remains in place, the cannabis industry will continue to struggle. The management team is filled with sharp people who know the life sciences REIT industry well. IIPR has invested in the preferred equity and the debt of IQHQ and not the common stock.

So, IIPR is cheap and well run, and I like it. I had a Strong Buy in August, when it was 20% of my model portfolio at 420 Investor, and the stock was a bit lower (near $47). I had initially rated it a Buy in December 2024 after the stock had crashed, happy that it was finally a Buy at $72.

What Concerns Me

At the core of the challenges IIPR faces is the financial health of its tenants. I have no near-term concerns about one of their tenants, Green Thumb Industries (GTBIF), which has low debt, but it has been just 9% of its rental income in the first three quarters of 2025. GTI is the second-largest tenant, behind Ascend Wellness, at 12% of the rent. Curaleaf (CURLF) was at 9%, while Trulieve (TCNNF) and Cannabist (CBSTF) were at 8%. I do have concerns about the other four MSO tenants, especially Curaleaf and Cannabist. If 280E taxation goes away (with cannabis getting rescheduled to Schedule III), it will be very good for the financials of these tenants.

I characterized the amount of debt as small, but the debt matures in May 2026. The senior notes were sold in 2021. When the company files its 10-K and has a conference call for its Q4 (in February, presumably), it may address this perhaps, though it could wait until the Q1 call in May, and it seems very likely that the interest-rate could rise from 5.5%. The company did repurchase some of the debt in February 2025, but it reported $291 million still outstanding as of 9/30. I assume that the rate will be going up, or the company would have already refinanced it. The company could also get a larger debt deal done, which would make the stock riskier in my view.

The current dividend yield is way too high at 15.2%. The $1.90 quarterly dividend exceeds the current adjusted funds from operations. I believe that the company should cut the dividend, but there has been no sign that it will do so. Perhaps as part of the debt refinancing, this will take place. I think that a dividend cut would be good for the stock in the long-run, but many investors might sell the stock.

Another concern of mine is that the company has been issuing preferred stock to repurchase common stock. I had previously mentioned this, but was glad to see the preferred stock holding. This is no longer the case, as the preferred stock, the Innovative Industrial Properties 9% Cumulative Preferred (IIPR.PR.A) has dropped more than 5% since I wrote about IIPR in December. It last traded at $22.74, which is below the $25.00 redemption price but above the low in April 2025 of $22.00. The volume has soared, and I think that IIPR investors should pay attention to this.

The cheap stock has gotten beat up for good reasons that include weak tenants (with many missed payments), maturing debt and a dividend that may be cut. The high-volume sell-off of the preferred stock is worrying me.

A Look At The Chart

IIPR posted a multi-year low in late November near $45. It is currently 12% higher. Here is the one-year chart:

As I pointed out above, the volume on the sinking preferred stock has been high recently. The volume on the common stock picked up in late December, when cannabis stocks were soaring on the potential rescheduling news, but it has declined in January.

IIPR is up 5.3% in 2026 so far, while the New Cannabis Ventures Global Cannabis Stock Index is unchanged at 6.59. The AdvisorShares Pure US Cannabis ETF (MSOS) is up 1.9%, closing at $4.81 on 1/13. The IIPR Preferred Stock has dropped 6.0%.

Taking a look at the action over the past five years, IIPR has outpaced MSOS:

Conclusion

While I have downgraded IIPR from Strong Buy to Hold, I still hold an 11.4% position in my model portfolio at 420 Investor, which is the same amount approximately as it was when I had it as Strong Buy. The model portfolio has 17% cash, which is below the maximum permitted of 20%, so I could trim more. The reality, though, is that for investors who want to be in the cannabis sector, IIPR remains a pretty good option.

For those who want to be in REITs, I have expressed a lot of concern about REITs, as I believe that they have far outpaced fixed-income, which is under pressure. I last wrote about this when I initiated a REIT ETF with a Sell rating and explaining why investors should be cautious.

While I don't cover the IIPR preferred stock (or any preferred stock), it looks cheap to me, and it might be a good substitute for holders of IIPR common. It is certainly safer than the common stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: HUB Group Only Makes Sense At Hard To Repeat Peak-Cycle Margins
data.description:
HUB Group (HUBG) is a mix of an intermodal trucking carrier and a trucking logistics company.

Despite its relatively higher exposure to lower asset-intensity segments (in logistics), the company has not earned very good returns on capital in past cycles, except for the 2022 peak, which seems unlikely to repeat.

At current prices, the stock already discounts relatively optimistic assumptions on normalized returns, while offering an earnings yield well below the market, and being unable to self-finance much growth. For these reasons, I initiate HUBG with a Hold.

Business intro

HUB Group operates in several segments of the trucking industry.

It generates roughly 55% of its revenues from intermodal and dedicated transportation, and about 45% from logistics. In terms of operating income, the mix is even more skewed toward logistics, which accounts for roughly 60% of total operating profits (based on the company's 10-K).

Intermodal focus on asset-heavy

The intermodal and dedicated segments make HUBG comparable, at least partially, to companies like J.B. Hunt (JBHT) or Schneider National (SNDR). However, HUB’s asset footprint tells a different story. The company operates only about 2,500 tractors, a very small number for a company of this revenue size. That fleet size is far more consistent with an intermodal-heavy model (primarily drayage and short-haul moves to and from rail ramps) than with long-distance truckload freight used in dedicated. In its investor presentation, the company shows only about 10% of revenues from dedicated, contrary to JBHT or SNDR, which have more focus on dedicated than intermodal.

Asset-light logistics

HUB’s logistics segment is diversified across several activities: brokerage (hiring of third-party carrier services, at ~40% of revenues), managed transportation (where HUB manages a customer’s supply chain more internally, at ~20% of revenues), consolidation and fulfillment (~20%), and last mile (~20%). All revenue figures come from the latest investor presentation.

Logistics margins are higher today and less volatile than those of intermodal and dedicated transportation. Today (based on the company's 10-K), logistics operates at roughly a 4.5% operating margin, compared to about 2.5% for intermodal and dedicated. At the 2022 peak, logistics margins reached around 6%, while intermodal and dedicated peaked at roughly 10.5%.

However, margins alone are not the key variable. What matters is how much capital is required to generate those margins. Logistics should, in principle, be less asset-intensive. Unfortunately, HUB Group does not disclose segment-level assets, which makes it impossible to calculate returns on capital by business line.

We can reasonably hypothesize that logistics earns better returns on assets than intermodal, but this remains an assumption.

Higher relative Mexico exposure

HUB Group’s exposure to Mexico is probably higher than that of most U.S. trucking and logistics peers, albeit not separately disclosed.

While the company does not disclose Mexico-related revenues explicitly, it is more concentrated in the western US (the company says it is the largest customer of Union Pacific Railroad, which operates from the Midwest to the West Coast), and describes itself as the largest cross-border intermodal operator.

This positioning benefits from nearshoring trends but also exposes the company to potential tariff disruptions and policy volatility in US–Mexico trade.

Capital allocation and capital returns

At the consolidated level, the company’s returns on assets have been modest.

Since around 2017, HUB Group has somewhat emphasized logistics, completing several acquisitions (four between 2017 and 2023, according to a recent presentation), to expand brokerage, managed transportation, and fulfillment capabilities, for ~$850 million in total.

The company does not disclose CAPEX or assets per segment, but we could speculate that intermodal and dedicated get the bulk of the bill, given their structurally higher asset requirements. The average has been $130 million since 2017, or ~$1.2 billion.

More recently, the company has also added intermodal exposure via acquisitions, including the acquisition of a 50% stake in EASO, a Mexican intermodal operator, for $55 million, and the acquisition of the refrigerated intermodal subsidiary from Marten Transport (MRTN) for $52 million.

Strategically, this suggests HUB is trying to balance its two segments (intermodal and logistics). The long-term results of these efforts are mixed. Outside of the pandemic period, consolidated operating returns on assets have generally ranged between 5% and 8%.

The spike during 21/22, observable in other trucking companies but particularly salient for HUBG, cannot be considered a 'normal' cycle, because of one-off factors like extreme supply chain dislocations, tight capacity, and stimulus-driven demand. I do not consider those conditions repeatable.

Overall, the quality of the business, as reflected in through-the-cycle returns on capital, does not appear particularly strong.

Governance and incentives

An additional factor worth highlighting is HUB Group’s governance structure. The company is chaired by a long-tenured executive who has been with the firm since 1975 and served as CEO until 2022. The current CEO is his son (10-K under Executive Officers).

Through Class B shares, the Chairman and CEO family holds effective voting control (with approximately 170 votes per Class B share versus one vote per Class A share), despite owning only about 2.5% of the company’s economic interest (all based on the latest proxy).

This structure materially reduces external shareholder influence and, in my view, lowers the probability of meaningful changes in capital allocation discipline.

Valuation is high for such a low return business

As has happened with other names in trucking, the company's stock has jumped 30%+ in recent months, probably on the basis of hope around a turnaround in the trucking cycle.

More than in other companies recently covered, I believe the current prices offer very little in terms of opportunity for potential shareholders.

Let us assume a relatively optimistic normalized scenario. If HUBG earns an average operating return on assets of 8% (a level only clearly achieved during the 2022 peak) on an asset base of roughly $2.9 billion, operating income would be about $232 million. With negligible interest expense and a 25% tax rate, net income would be approximately $175 million.

Against a current market capitalization of about $2.85 billion, this implies a valuation of roughly 16x normalized earnings, or an earnings yield of just over 6%. That is meaningfully below the long-term return of the equity market as a whole, for a company that has a very poor capital return history.

Furthermore, growth does not materially improve the picture. At an 8% pre-tax return on assets (about 6% post-tax), the company can only grow assets at roughly 6% by fully reinvesting earnings, in my view. In that case, growth simply consumes the entire earnings yield, leaving shareholders with an aggregate return of around 6%. Again, under fairly optimistic assumptions.

If we reduced the cycle-average return on assets to 6% pre-tax, then the earnings yield falls to 4.5% ($130 million in net income), and the post-tax return on assets (4.5% exactly) would only allow for growth at that level, again offering a total of only 4.5% in return.

At current prices, the stock offers a subpar earnings yield with little margin of safety. For these reasons, I do not find HUBG attractive today and initiate coverage with a Hold.

data.title: Netflix Q4 2025 Earnings Preview: Warner Bros. Discovery Bid Takes Priority
data.description:
The Netflix stock (NFLX) has yet to recover from its post-earnings selloff since late October when it reported Q3 2025 results, underscoring continued investors’ angst around the durability of its premium. The initial post-earnings pullback was largely due to fundamental underperformance resulting from a tax dispute settlement with Brazilian authorities that represented a one-time $619 million headwind.

Netflix’s Q3 revenue also fell short of Wall Street estimates, reflecting concerns I’ve raised in my previous coverage over Netflix’s increasing exposure to average revenue per member (“ARM”) dilution risks. This is especially relevant as incremental paid subscription adds skew toward lower-earning regions like Asia. Inherently price-elastic consumers are also increasingly opting into the ad-supported tier, which commands a lower subscription rate. Although management continues to emphasize long-term monetization upside from the ad-supported tier as Netflix’s ad inventory ramps up, the offering remains structurally ARM-dilutive. This effectively heightens execution risks ahead, as Netflix’s strategy in countering stalling subscriber adds with accelerated user base monetization remains challenged in the near-term.

The company’s latest bid for Warner Bros. Discovery’s (WBD) Streaming & Studios (“S&S”) operations also adds complexity to the durability of the Netflix stock’s current premium. Specifically, Netflix’s core operations are unlikely to see material near-term upside catalysts, since recent growth initiatives – spanning advertising, gaming and podcasts – remain in early execution. Given the evolving dynamics of the impending WBD S&S acquisition – especially with the deal structure potentially still in flux amid the Paramount Skydance (PSKY) hostile offer – transaction details are likely to dominate investors’ focus ahead of earnings.

The Proposed Netflix And Warner Bros. Discovery Merger

Netflix announced on December 5th that it’s entered into an agreement to acquire WBD’s S&S operations for a total equity value of $72 billion – or enterprise value of $82.7 billion – which implies absorption of about $10.7 billion in debt. Under the initially accepted terms by WBD, the transaction consideration will consist of both cash and equity.

Specifically, the deal consideration translates to $27.75 per share to existing WBD shareholders, which will be funded by $23.25 in cash and $4.50 in Netflix shares at the time of completion. However, the equity portion of the deal consideration is subject to change, given the collar arrangement in place. WBD shareholders are only entitled to $4.50 in Netflix shares only if Netflix continues to trade at a 15-day volume weighted average price (“VWAP”) between $97.91 and $119.67 three days before the transaction’s completion. If Netflix’s VWAP in the three days before deal closing falls below $97.71, then the equity consideration due to WBD shareholders will be pro-rated to 0.046 Netflix shares for each WBD share – or below $4.50 in Netflix shares. Likewise, if Netflix’s VWAP exceeds $119.67 three days before the transaction’s close, then WBD shareholders will be entitled to incremental upside at 0.0376 Netflix shares – or more than $4.50 in Netflix shares.

Since Netflix’s current price already falls below the low range of the collar, the equity portion of the deal consideration now translates to about $4.23 in Netflix Shares (i.e., 0.046 x 15-day VWAP ~$92.01). This introduces more than $10.5 billion in market cap (~2.5%) dilution to the Netflix stock, based on its current price of $90.32 (January 13 close).

However, Netflix shareholders will likely argue that this is far from a deal. The stock’s value has plummeted more than 27% since the Q3 earnings release, and almost 10% since the WBD transaction was announced in early December. This downtrend likely underscores growing investors’ angst as Netflix’s immediate operational execution challenges risk being compounded by added deal complexities in the near-term.

Why Netflix Wants WBD's Streaming & Studios So Bad

As discussed in my recent coverage on WBD, Netflix’s proposed transaction price for S&S embeds a significant valuation premium. My base case forecast projects WBD Streaming and Studios’ respective 2026 adjusted EBITDA at $1.7 billion and $2.7 billion. I assume a Studios' valuation at 11.9x NTM EV/EBITDA, which is in line with the current industry average (discussed here). My valuation assumptions also pro-rate net debt of $10.7 billion, as implied by Netflix’s offer, between the two segments’ anticipated sales mix. Under a reverse sum-of-the-parts valuation analysis, Netflix’s current offer at $27.75 per WBD share implies a 28x NTM EV/EBITDA for WBD Streaming. This represents a significant premium on a relative basis to its streaming peers’, which are expected to deliver greater EBITDA durability over the longer-term:

i. Streaming Projections

ii. Studios Projections

iii. Reverse SOTP Analysis for S&S

iv. Streaming Peer Comp

Yet, both Netflix and WBD management remain adamant on completing the proposed deal, despite recent headwinds from PSKY’s hostile tender offer. Under the currently proposed deal structure, Netflix has the option to match any competing offers that WBD deems to be superior. As discussed in my recent coverage on WBD’s impending sale, Netflix management likely has the intention to do so if needed. This is corroborated by management’s affirmation that the company will be able to maintain its investment grade credit rating and shareholder capital returns program, despite expectations for “pro-forma leverage to be elevated at closing”. The company also has a robust balance sheet from existing operations to support any changes to the WBD bid.

Deal financing considerations aside, Netflix management also remains optimistic about the post-close synergies of the deal.

Business Expansion

Specifically, management’s committed to extending Netflix’s reach into “businesses and capabilities” – such as theatrical releases – that it’s historically had no exposure in through the WBD S&S acquisition. And despite PSKY’s continued scrutiny of its offer, Netflix remains committed to the proposed merger with WBD S&S.

In a recent response to PSKY’s critique on the Netflix-WBD tie-up’s potential regulatory challenges, Netflix’s Co-CEO Greg Peters has cited confidence that the deal is “pro-consumer” and will likely gain approval from authorities. In a follow-up Form 425 filing last week, Netflix has also disclosed that it’s already submitted the Hart-Scott-Rodino filing pertaining to the upcoming acquisition of WBD S&S operations, and is “engaging with competition authorities, including the U.S. Department of Justice and European Commission” – matching pre-merger efforts that PSKY said it’s done a step ahead to “secure all necessary regulatory approvals expeditiously.”

Accelerating Paid Subscriptions

Netflix’s commitment to the acquisition of WBD’s S&S operations likely stems from the anticipated growth synergies – especially as the combination could reinforce its global streaming leadership and, more importantly, monetization prospects through streaming ads. As discussed in the previous coverage, Netflix’s subscription growth has stalled in its most profitable geographies like UCAN due to increasing maturity of its platform in the region alongside intensifying competition. The acquisition of WBD S&S can improve its content library and potentially reaccelerate its global paid subscription adds – especially in high-earning regions like UCAN.

Specifically, HBO Max is not yet available in Canada, despite its expanded availability to more than 100 countries. And in the U.S., it only commands a nominal share of TV screen time at 1.3%, highlighting significant growth headroom in high-earning regions for Netflix. Given Netflix’s developed global tech stack, it can be leveraged by HBO Max to accelerate expansion, underscoring additive subscription growth opportunities post-close.

Advertising

More importantly, the impending merger could increase Netflix’s ad share, which is critical to its current efforts in optimizing subscriber base monetization. Netflix’s ad-supported tier has gained robust reception since its launch, now boasting more than 190 million monthly active users (“MAU”) globally. And the option remains a key subscription growth driver for the company, despite it being ARM dilutive still.

Comparatively, HBO Max has been one of the pioneers in implementing ad-supported streaming, with related revenue growth being a key contributor to its earnings inflection in recent quarters. The streaming platform, under WBD’s management, has also “maintained a very strong price premium for the quality of (ad inventory)” at industry’s latest Upfront ad sales.

This proves favourable to Netflix’s current growth focus, as the addition of HBO Max could meaningfully expand its total ad inventory while also accelerating its access to a more mature ad deliver tech stack. The set-up would effectively improve Netflix’s positioning to capitalize on a larger share of accelerating streaming ad budgets amid the secular shift to digital placements. Specifically, streaming ad placements now represent 15% of total long-form video advertising spending worldwide; related streaming ad budgets are expected to have grown 15% y/y in the U.S. alone. The dynamics underscore how the impending acquisition of WBD S&S could bolster Netflix’s competitive advantage against ad-supported streaming rivals like Disney+ (DIS) and Amazon Prime Video (AMZN).

Ancillary Benefits

The impending merger could also draw ancillary benefits to Netflix’s emerging gaming strategy. As discussed in my previous coverage on the stock, the company’s increasing focus on diversifying its growth portfolio has led it to double-down on gaming investments. In addition to the continued introduction of new games based on Netflix Original IPs like Stranger Things and Squid Game, the company’s also expanded from its previous mobile-only strategy to offering TV-based gameplay as well. The upcoming acquisition of WBD’s S&S operations could further broaden Netflix’s access to high-value gaming IPs, including WBD’s “Hogwarts Legacy” which continues to garner strong reception following its 2023 release.

Cost Tailwinds

In addition to the foregoing analysis of potential growth synergies, the transaction could also yield cost efficiencies for Netflix’ expansion roadmap. Specifically, Netflix’s disclosed its intentions to realize “at least $2 billion to $3 billion of annual cost savings by the third year post closing”, which will drive GAAP EPS accretion at the consolidated level by the second year following the transaction’s completion. Most of these savings will come from reductions in overlapping back-end expenses, like generative and administrative spend, while Netflix remains committed to maintaining WBD Studios’ current focus on theatrical releases. This is consistent with the foregoing analysis of a mutually beneficial relationship, in which both parties could potentially optimize returns on their existing investments. Netflix could leverage HBO Max’s more advanced ad delivery tech stack to strengthen its ongoing monetization efforts, while HBO Max benefits from Netflix’s global streaming infrastructure to accelerate capitalization of its international growth opportunities.

Implications Of An All-Cash Offer

In the latest development, Netflix’s reportedly mulling an all-cash offer for WBD S&S instead of the current cash-equity deal structure. This comes amid PSKY’s commitment to its tender offer for WBD shares.

Specifically, WBD has released its second recommendation against PSKY’s amended offer last week, reaffirming its stance that the Netflix deal remains superior. In addition, WBD highlights its exposure to a $4.7 billion cost headwind if it abandons the existing Netflix deal, which it wouldn’t be compensated for in a worst-case scenario where the hypothetical PSKY replacement deal falls through as well:

The total cost to WBD would be approximately $4.7 billion, or $1.79 per share. These costs would, in effect, lower the net amount of the regulatory termination fee that PSKY would pay to WBD from $5.8 billion to $1.1 billion in the event of a failed transaction with PSKY. In comparison, the Netflix transaction imposes none of these costs on WBD.

Source: Warner Bros. Discovery Board Of Directors Unanimously Recommends Shareholders Reject Amended Paramount Tender Offer, January 6, 2026

In response, PSKY has launched a lawsuit against WBD for alleged violation of Delaware law. Based on PSKY’s latest press release, its Chairman and CEO David Ellison has pointed to how WBD has “never said because it cannot, (that) the Netflix transaction is financially superior” to PSKY’s offer.

More importantly, he highlights limited transparency related to WBD’s valuation for the upcoming Global Networks spinoff and Netflix transaction, which is against Delaware law:

Along with the WBD shareholders, we have asked for the customary financial disclosure a board is supposed to provide shareholders when making an investment recommendation. But in each of its 14D-9 filings, WBD has failed to include any disclosure about how it valued the Global Networks stub equity, how it valued the overall Netflix transaction, how the purchase price reduction for debt works in the Netflix transaction, or even what the basis is for its "risk adjustment" of our $30 per share all-cash offer. WBD shareholders need this information to make an informed investment decision on our offer – and importantly, Delaware law has consistently required that such information be provided to shareholders.

Source: PARAMOUNT PROVIDES UPDATE TO WARNER BROS. DISCOVERY SHAREHOLDERS, January 12, 2026

Given the coincidence in timing with recent reports on Netflix’s consideration of changing its WBD bid to an all-cash offer, it could be a move to strategically bolster the impending transaction’s financial superiority and counter PSKY’s challenge.

However, the restructuring of its bid to an all-cash offer could further compound looming fundamental headwinds for Netflix. Specifically, the existing offer for WBD has already exposed Netflix to $59 billion of new financing in the form of a bridge loan – the largest of its kind. A portion of which has already recently been refinanced into $20 billion of delayed draw term loans and $5 billion revolving credit facility, with the remainder to be converted into longer-term debt as well post-close. The potential shift to an all-cash bid could introduce Netflix to an additional upfront investment burden and compound execution risks. It could also potentially offset the proposed deal synergies discussed in the earlier sections, since an all-cash offer could increase Netflix’s leverage and finance cost exposure.

The emerging risk has been evident in the Netflix stock’s continued selloff since the announcement of the impending acquisition. And the potential shift to an all-cash offer could further the stock’s risk premium. This effectively leaves limited room for error at Netflix’s upcoming earnings, given investors’ increased expectations for an uplift to its growth and earnings profile to justify the potential uptick in anticipated cash outflow and leverage exposure.

WBD Bid Adds Valuation Pressure Ahead

More importantly, the impending WBD acquisition is likely to be value-dilutive for Netflix in every scenario from pre-merger to post-close. As discussed earlier, Netflix is offering a significant premium for WBD’s S&S operations. This has likely already priced in the anticipated deal synergies, and leaves little room to absorb Netflix’s imminent exposure elevated execution risks ahead:

Pre-Merger

The proposed deal’s regulatory uncertainties remain a key overhang on Netflix’s valuation outlook. During the early days of the deal’s announcement, President Trump has vowed personal involvement in the transaction’s regulatory process and cited risks of “potential concentration of market power in the entertainment industry”. And just over the weekend (January 11), President Trump’s referenced an article titled “Stop the Netflix Cultural Takeover”, which “argues against the Netflix offer” for WBD. The impending regulatory hurdle could be an added distraction to Netflix at an inopportune time, given execution challenges in its core existing operations.

Specifically, Netflix has committed to a deal termination fee of $5.8 billion to WBD if the transaction falls through due to regulatory scrutiny. The deal also has an “outside date” – or maximum timeline to completion – of 21 months. This accordingly risks exposing Netflix to an extended period of volatility, as expectations for up to 21 months of tough regulatory scrutiny over the deal could incentivize the market to price in the termination cost headwind.

In addition to regulatory distraction, the upcoming PSKY lawsuit against WBD could be similarly adverse to Netflix. Since PSKY’s case is built to require WBD disclosure of details pertaining to its evaluation of the Netflix offer, any court-ordered release of related information could introduce incremental uncertainty to the economics of the impending acquisition. This could accordingly invite greater investors’ scrutiny over the transaction’s financial implications on Netflix’s operations, and subject the stock to further volatility.

Initial Post-Close

Even if the deal gains regulatory approval to completion, the impending integration costs and challenges could extend the Netflix stock’s exposure to downside risks. As mentioned earlier, management doesn’t expect earnings accretion from the deal until two years after close. The anticipated annual cost synergies of up to $3 billion also aren’t expected until three years after the transaction’s done. This represents an extended timeline that could expose Netflix to heightened post-close execution risks. This would align with historical observations from the April 2022 merger between Warner Media and Discovery, whereby initial expectations for $52 billion in sales and $14 billion in EBITDA within one year post-close were essentially thrown out the window after just months of the completed transaction.

The risk of this repeating remains on the table, as both Netflix and WBD’s S&S unit has been operating under very different visions. This was recently acknowledged by Netflix management during the M&A call for the WBD acquisition:

So a reminder, we've released about 30 films into theatres this year. So it sounds like we have this opposition to movies in the theatres. My pushback has been mostly in the fact of the long exclusive windows, which we don't really think of that consumer friendly. But when we talk about keeping HBO operating largely as it is, it also includes their output movie deal with Warner Bros, which includes life cycle that starts in the movie theatre, which we're going to continue to support.

So I don't -- I wouldn't look at this as a change in approach for Netflix movies or for Warner movies for that matter. I think over time, I think the windows will evolve to be much more consumer-friendly to be able to meet the audience where they are quicker, all those things we'd like to do.

Source: Netflix M&A Call Transcript, December 5, 2025

But making this work could take a lot of resources and a long time, considering WBD CEO David Zaslav’s years of effort in converting HBO Max and its Studios operations back to the traditional “windowing” strategy – which diverged from Warner Media’s prior endorsement of the “Project Popcorn” direct-to-streaming approach employed under then-CEO Jason Kilar’s leadership. Now Netflix likely wants to bring S&S back to where it started, given focus on making theatrical releases “more consumer-friendly” – albeit not entirely, given the weak ROI on simultaneous streaming and theatrical releases, and likely closer to something like the limited 16-day theatrical run for “Wake Up Dead Man: A Knives Out Mystery” before it was released on Netflix streaming. This would also be consistent with recent speculation that Netflix is considering a “17-day theatrical release window” after the WBD acquisition.

The strategic shift for S&S would be the first of its kind in the rapidly evolving industry, potentially making it an additional execution hurdle for Netflix. As a result, reservations are warranted on the deal synergy realization timeline currently proposed by Netflix management – which the stock’s current premium likely hasn’t sufficiently de-risked for.

Long-Term Post-Close Execution Risks

As discussed earlier, the key tailwind to Netflix’s acquisition of WBD’s S&S operations stems from the extended scale and reach into digital ad dollars and global paid subscription opportunities. However, there’s also significant overlap between Netflix and HBO Max’s target audience that needs to be considered, which management’s also acknowledged. There are two main post-close operating alternatives for the two platforms, with clear trade-offs that aren’t adequately reflected in the Netflix stock’s current premium, as well as the premium it’s offered for WBD S&S:

1. Maintaining Netflix And HBO Max As Separate Platforms

This is likely what Netflix intends to do in the early stages of S&S integration, based on management’s sentiment during the M&A call. By keeping the two separate, Netflix could appease antitrust requirements by maintaining competition in the marketplace. It could also allow better monetization of HBO Max by leveraging Netflix’s global infrastructure to further the platform’s currently restricted international reach.

But this could also imply that Netflix’s subscription growth could remain at status quo, with increased exposure to ARM-dilutive adds from Asia and Latin America, in particular. Under separate operations, the impending uplift to Netflix’s long-term ad growth would essentially be “acquired” from HBO Max’s incremental ad inventory, rather than synergetic to its existing trajectory. And given the premium Netflix’s paying for WBD’s S&S operations, the dynamic could risk capping incremental value creation in the long-run.

2. Combining Netflix And HBO Max

This is likely something Netflix is currently mulling for the long-run. This would be consistent with management’s recent allusion to potential bundling and/or tiering opportunities between the two platforms to unlock incremental value from HBO Max – although it’s still something that they’re still “figuring out”:

We've also seen that sort of some of these bundles and models, if you construct them correctly, have all sorts of benefits, retention benefits, engagement benefits as well, but there's also a lot of value in the flip side of this. So you can think about the many, many Netflix members around the world who are not currently HBO Max subscribers and are not getting any value from that HBO Max titles, Warner Bros. titles.

And there's a real opportunity we think of actually figuring out how do we bring more of those titles in the right way through some combination of plans and tiering, et cetera, to unlock the value in those assets.

Source: Netflix M&A Call Transcript, December 5, 2025

This option is likely where the bulk of the deal’s impending growth synergies could emerge from – but not without its challenges, as Netflix and HBO Max’s significant subscriber overlap could introduce extended periods of churn in the event of a combination. It also comes at a time when the streaming and broader media and entertainment industry prioritizes “MVPDs” – or multichannel video programming distributor streaming, typically represented by bundled offers. This was first introduced through Disney’s multiple in-house streaming offerings, spanning Disney+, Hulu and ESPN+, and gained popularity in the past year due to intensifying market competition. Notable streaming bundles introduced in 2025 include the Apple TV (AAPL) and Peacock (CMCSA) tie-up at $15 a month, as well as the sports-focused Fox (FOX / FOXA) and ESPN streaming combo introduced in the summer that left HBO Max out after the “Venu” project failed.

Heightening competition from industry bundling dynamics could temper Netflix and HBO Max’s combination synergies. Although MVPD arrangements implemented by WBD to date have outperformed expectations and garnered “better ARPU”, most of the upside has come from opportunities to upsell to its premium ad-free, standalone HBO Max offering:

And frankly, in all of -- some of our biggest, most recent partnerships, both in the U.S. and outside the U.S., we are trending above what we expected in terms of activations…And then the other thing that we've seen that has been very healthy and also leads to better ARPU is in most all of those deals, we have upsell capabilities. And so we go from an ad-lite product to an ability to actually upsell the customer where we take the majority of the economics on the upsell to ad-free, which also has an ability to drive more ARPU for us, particularly outside the U.S., where ad sales, obviously, is still a growth business, but it's starting off a lower base.

Source: WBD Q2 2025 Earnings Call Transcript

Yet, this upselling opportunity may be tempered for Netflix after it merges with HBO Max. While it can leverage the combined content libraries to engage in different tiering opportunities to better price gauge consumer demand, the dynamics of internalizing HBO Max could limit Netflix’s post-close benefit from industry’s current bundling effect.

Potential Upside Drivers To Consider

Despite the ongoing deal overhang, there are still potential upside considerations that could propel a near-term uplift for the Netflix stock.

This includes potential for better-than-expected performance at its upcoming Q4 2025 earnings release. The period covers momentum-driving titles, including the two-part release of Stranger Things Season 5 Finale in November and December, alongside the typical holiday season tailwind that could imply a subscription and ad sales boost. Looking ahead, the company could also benefit from a potential uplift ahead of the upcoming February 2026 Olympics, with the impending release of related sports docuseries programming that could boost subscriptions and ad placements.

While this could drive an uplift to the stock, especially given its extended selloff in recent months, the upside potential is unlikely durable. This would be consistent with the stock’s ongoing exposure to incremental deal execution risks, which span regulatory, legal and fundamental uncertainties.

Final Thoughts

Taken together, I believe Netflix’s upcoming acquisition of WBD’s S&S operations could introduce incremental execution risks, potentially skewing the stock towards my downside scenario price target near $80 per share. Investors will likely demand substantial fundamental outperformance at its upcoming earnings to justify the stock’s current premium and compensate for the potentially intensifying deal risk exposure ahead. More importantly, extended uncertainties over the impending transaction’s consideration structure and closing, given the 21-month outside window, is likely to remain a persistent overhang on the stock, which its current premium has yet to sufficiently de-risk for.

data.title: Beyond AI: Industrial Sector Investment Themes To Watch
data.description:
AI investment has dominated the industrial space, but according to Juliana Faircloth, Vice President and Director for Portfolio Research with TD Asset Management, new themes are emerging which may continue to propel the sector forward.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: Massive amounts of AI spending dominating the industrial space, but are we beginning to see other themes emerging for the sector? Joining us now to discuss is Juliana Faircloth, VP and Director for Portfolio Research with TD Asset Management.

Great to have you back on the show.

Juliana Faircloth: Thank you for having me. Nice to see you.

Greg Bonnell: Nice to see you too. This has been an age where we've been dominated by AI headlines. It is also true in terms of the amount of money it's raising. And perhaps some of the money is taking away from other spaces. Talk to me about what's been happening in the industrial space, beneath the surface of all this.

Juliana Faircloth: Well, it's no secret that the AI CapEx trend has really dominated for the last, I would say, 18-24 months. We've seen that play out through really strong performance within industrials and electrical equipment, power equipment, grid infrastructure, cooling.

But I think this paints sort of a misleading picture of what's really going on in the industrial economy. So, while the AI CapEx has been very strong, catching a lot of headlines, catching a lot of investment, the rest of the industrial economy will, say, has been struggling with a few different things.

So they've been struggling with deferred CapEx. So, all of this political uncertainty, tariff uncertainty has kept a lot of companies on the sidelines from really investing. They've been dealing with an inventory overhang that's needed to be digested. So, lots of orders throughout the pandemic eventually shipped, built up too much inventory. That all needed to be worked through the system. And they've also been dealing with just generally cautious customer behavior.

So you've really actually seen quite a bifurcation within the industrial market. I brought a chart that I think helps.

Greg Bonnell: Now show us the chart.

Juliana Faircloth: --show that quite nicely. So this chart, you'll see the blue line is the S&P 500 Electrical Equipment Subindex, performance over the last two years really strong. So companies in there, like Eaton (ETN), GE Vernova (GEV), lots of familiar names.

And comparing this here with the S&P 500 Transportation Index-- so rails, trucking-- sort of meant to reflect that kind of general industrial spend type of index. So you'll see quite a divergence here over the last 18-24 months within industrials.

Greg Bonnell: Alright, so that green line-- transportation-- a little starved of attention, maybe a little starved of investment as well as all the money goes towards AI. What's the setup then for 2026?

Juliana Faircloth: So, I think AI is not a theme that is going to be stopped this year. We know the hyperscalers are going to spend something like $600 billion in CapEx this year. But I think if we look at the rest of the industrial economy, sort of breaking out that AI piece, there's sort of a micro and macro story going on.

So from a macro perspective, we have rate cuts. We have tax cuts from the OBBBA. Tariff rates have probably peaked. So generally pretty supportive of a more cyclical recovery and backdrop. And even in the handful of trading days we've had so far this year, we've seen some strong performance of what we think of traditionally as cyclicals, so industrials as a sector as a whole, but also consumer discretionary materials.

But then from a more sector-specific micro perspective, we are seeing some interesting setup for a broader industrial recovery. So, generally, the fleet age of machinery is very, very old. If you think trucks, airplanes, tractors-- all of these pieces of equipment kind of started off as being delayed CapEx in COVID. That has sort of compounded over the last five or six years into a structurally old fleet.

You're also being bottlenecked by maintenance backlogs. So all of these old pieces of equipment need a lot more servicing, they need a lot more repair. That's now becoming a backlog that's impacting productivity for manufacturers.

And then lastly, what I think is emerging as an interesting trend is just a push towards productivity. The labor market's quite tight. The manufacturing labor market is quite tight. And so that's pushing companies to consider investing in things like automation and new machinery to help improve the productivity of their output.

So, that sort of micro and macro together is an interesting setup for a broader industrial CapEx outlook for 2026.

Greg Bonnell: So if we do start getting that broader CapEx spending across the industrial space, what parts of the industrial economy stand to benefit?

Juliana Faircloth: There's a few. I think a few to highlight which might be interesting would be mining equipment, industrial automation, and maybe transportation, as we've kind of highlighted it here already.

So, to start with mining equipment, I think you're dealing with a combination here of an elevated fleet age, as we've talked about, but also a very strong commodity and end-market backdrop. So, companies in the mining equipment space have been highlighting structurally older mining equipment fleets. So a company like Sandvik has said they're seeing the oldest fleet they've seen in the last several decades, so certainly ripe for a replacement cycle.

At the same time, as I think we all know and the audience is well aware, commodities have been really strong. So the backdrop for investment for mining companies is pretty interesting. I think the level of visibility of demand for some of the commodities, like copper from electrification, creates a more comfortable environment for mining companies to consider reinvesting and deploying a little bit of CapEx.

So you've started to see orders inflect a little bit in these areas, at companies like Caterpillar (CAT), or Toromont (TMTNF) and Finning (FINGF) in Canada, and some of the European players as well.

Greg Bonnell: That's some of the miners. What about other spaces? You got a few more for us?

Juliana Faircloth: I do. So industrial automation, I think, is another sort of interesting pocket to think about. Again, we're thinking here about a bit of an end-market recovery, as well as this push towards productivity and sort of automation.

So, from an end-market perspective, automation and robotics generally tied to broad industrial production and manufacturing. Autos are a particularly significant end market, at least today, for automation. If you or any of the viewers have been and toured through some of those auto plants, there's a lot of painting robots and a lot of mechanical arms, all of which have been pretty depressed with auto production being quite weak over the last couple of years.

So as we start to see some end-market recovery tied with a push towards reinvesting in automation across manufacturing plants, helping to enhance the productivity of the limited existing labor within the manufacturing economy, it sets up nicely for industrial automation companies.

So some of the large European conglomerates have a lot of exposure there. Like a Siemens, or ABB, or Schneider have seen some bottoming in their industrial automation businesses.

Greg Bonnell: So that's mining and industrial automation. Was there a third one?

Juliana Faircloth: There was a third one.

Greg Bonnell: There was a third one.

Juliana Faircloth: Transportation. We've been in a pretty difficult freight recession for the last three years. It's something I think you and I have talked about a few times. It's been a tough environment for rails, and it's been a tough environment for the trucking companies.

As we start to see that broad sort of industrial muscle start to move a little bit more positively throughout the economy, it's definitely a good setup for the rails, which we can think of as kind of the veins of the industrial system really moving everything from point A to point B.

So we've already seen that in truck rates starting to tighten up. And it sets up for perhaps the end of this quite prolonged freight recession that the rails and transports have been dealing with.

Greg Bonnell: It would perhaps suggest to me that there might be some risks there. What are the risks?

Juliana Faircloth: Absolutely. So there's always risks. I think the first and probably largest risk is just political and geopolitical uncertainty. We're still operating in quite a volatile time, from a policy perspective and from a geopolitical perspective. And that can be quite impinging on companies' confidence and willingness to invest and put capital behind some of their decisions and behind their business.

I think a general economic slowdown would obviously be negative for some of these more cyclical and cyclically driven sectors. And then lastly, AI is a very hungry machine. And that could continue to sort of crowd out some investment in more traditional or broader industrial spending as we continue to fuel that AI fire.

Original Post

data.title: I Loved Prologis, But It's Time To Say Goodbye (Downgrade)
data.description:
Prologis (PLD) has outperformed many REITs in large part due to the resilience of the logistics growth story. Now, management appears to be targeting a new frontier in data centers. I still have great confidence in the company’s growth trajectory, as warehouses may benefit from secular tailwinds for decades to come. It is mainly the valuation that now concerns me, as the stock does not look compelling relative to alternatives. I am downgrading the stock to a neutral rating.

PLD Stock Price

I last covered PLD in June, where I upgraded the stock to a "Buy" rating after five years of underperformance. The stock has risen around 26% inclusive of dividends, outpacing the broader market.

It may seem hasty to some, but it is time to downgrade.

PLD Stock Key Metrics

PLD is one of the largest logistics real estate investment trusts ("REITs") in the world. This real estate landlord leases warehouses across the world, with the majority of its presence seen in the United States and Europe.

I view warehouses as being one of the highest-quality sub-sectors in the real estate space. Warehouses are mission-critical infrastructure that are necessary to connect the dots between production and sale.

PLD appears to own one of the higher-quality logistics portfolios, as it has focused on areas with higher income density.

Those ideas all sound nice, but my experience in investing (especially with mall REITs) is that just because something looks or sounds nice does not mean that it is a good business. From a numbers perspective, I can state one clear reason why PLD is positioned for long-term growth. Warehouse assets tend to have rent rates well below market, and PLD is no exception, as they estimate that their market spread to in-place rents stands at around 20%.

This can help explain how the company has continued to show stellar growth rates, with same-store NOI jumping 5.2% YoY. The company saw leasing spreads come in at an impressive 29.4%.

These led to core FFO jumping 3.4% YoY to $1.50 per share (excluding net promote income). That growth rate is strong when compared to REIT peers, as growth has been elusive due to the higher interest rate environment.

Management has guided the full year to see up to $5.86 in core FFO per share, representing 6% YoY growth.

PLD ended the quarter with a solid balance sheet with leverage at 5x debt to EBITDA. This isn’t the strongest balance sheet in the sector - Terreno Realty (TRNO) stands out on this metric - but I see little concern with this amount of leverage given both the resilient income stream and the secular growth drivers.

Management has made a meaningful shift in their investment plans. Whereas management has a long and successful track record of developing warehouse assets, they now view data centers (presumably for AI) as the next growth frontier. Management believes that data centers offer higher yields, higher margins, and higher investment potential.

Management calls this a “generational value creation opportunity” that they anticipate will create as much as $25 billion in value over the next decade.

It is understandable why management may be attracted to this opportunity, given that warehouse assets have tended to feature development yields of “only” 6.8%, with the value creation being the spread between that and the projected 5.2% stabilized cap rate.

On the conference call, management implied around $5 billion of potential annual investment in data centers, which might equate to “about 150 basis points of additional growth per annum.” Management believes that “power will be the constraint,” stating that it “won't be capital.” My opinion is that we might want to see results on a long term basis before getting too excited. I wonder if the exuberance of the AI euphoria may be spreading to real estate, with the risk being PLD investing heavily in data center real estate only to see its counterparties go bankrupt prior to completion.

I note that PLD’s balance sheet is in good shape and that my take may prove to be overly cynical, with my intended takeaway mainly being to remain cautious on projected growth assumptions. Regarding their core warehouse portfolio, management noted there are reasons to be optimistic about occupancy growth. Management expects demand to normalize, leading to a “recovery opportunity.” Management noted that they “enter this new phase of the market at a substantially lower market vacancy as compared to prior cycles,” which may allow them more pricing power than in the past.

Is PLD Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

At recent prices, PLD traded at a 3.1% forward dividend yield.

That represents significant compression since I last looked at it, when it traded at a 3.8% yield. I expect the company to sustain solid growth moving forward. I may not be as bullish as others regarding the data center opportunity, but perhaps we might be looking at 3% to 5% growth from the core warehouse assets with the potential for some more if the data center investments work out. If one compares to where the stock has traded just a handful of years ago, the setup might look attractive. However, I was not so bullish on the stock when it traded at such aggressive dividend yields and am even less so today. Net lease REITs like Realty Income (O) are trading weakly at 5% to 6% yields. I also recently wrote a piece regarding the preferred stock of Innovative Industrial Properties (IIPR), which is currently yielding nearly 10%.

PLD still has some upside to my previous target of a 3% yield, but I view the potential upside as insufficient relative to the aforementioned bargains seen elsewhere in the REIT sector. I think there’s an underestimated possibility that PLD does not see its yield compress further. In this scenario, the stock might be priced for just around 8% to 9% returns, but that is assuming that it can achieve the high end of my growth assumptions while experiencing no yield expansion.

PLD Stock Conclusion

PLD is one of the rare true growth names in the REIT sector, and its foray into data centers might help it to retain that title. Investors may want to pay close attention to how the investments in data centers play out, as I think some skepticism may be warranted. It is mainly the valuation that has led to my downgrade. At Best of Breed Growth Stocks, I have preferred net lease REITs and especially IIPR (and its preferred stock). I am downgrading PLD to a "Neutral" rating.

data.title: TTM Technologies: The Tailwinds Will Continue To Push Share Price, But I'll Pass
data.description:
Introduction

TTM Technologies, Inc. (TTMI) had an amazing start to the year and even a better 2025, so I wanted to look into the company’s performance in more detail and see what is still ahead for it. I don’t think the share price appreciation is going to stop right now, given the sentiment and overall exuberance, but from a modelling perspective, there’s very little upside. The company will have to report perfect numbers to continue to see its share price appreciate as it has in the past.

Briefly on Performance

Over the past couple of years, the company saw its sales grow at a somewhat acceptable pace when we look at the 2024 numbers, ranging from around 4%-to 14% in Q4 ’24. However, all of that changed in Q2 ’25, when its sales began to grow at over 20% compared to the same time last year. It seems that the company’s products really began to surge in demand across its two main revenue segments, the Aerospace & Defense, as well as data center computing, which both saw sales increase over 20% y/y.

This makes sense. 2025 has been a massive year for these two segments. Geopolitical conflicts around the globe continued to push budget increases on national security spending, as well as the massive AI race that has been raging on across the hyperscalers. These tailwinds pushed the company’s sales to new highs, surpassing its previous best year of FY17 on a TTM basis.

The most recent quarter showed the company grew its sales by 22% y/y and a quarterly record high in diluted EPS of $0.67. Looking at the company’s margins over the same period, we can see a steady improvement throughout, boosted by more efficient business operations on the back of increased demand for its products, allowing it to leverage its fixed cost structure more effectively. Additionally, a lot of the demand came from high-density interconnect, or HDI PCBs for Gen AI, which naturally come with much higher profitability compared to commoditized boards.

With an increased bottom line comes an increase in other profitability and efficiency metrics; however, they are still quite unappealing. The uptrend that we see is positive, and I do believe that over the next few quarters or even the year, these will double. They may not double if the company increases its Capex spending, but at least the uptrend is going to remain.

Let’s take a look at the company’s financial position. Cash and equivalents stood at around $491m against $913m in long-term debt. $500m of the debt at 4% is due in three years, and that is the closest substantial due date. Interest expense for the recent quarter stood at around $10m, which is easily covered by the company’s EBIT, the mountain of cash, and operating cash flow. Free cash flow for this quarter, however, was only $5m (for the three quarters to date) due to a massive increase in capex. I think this is a positive since the company is preparing for the demand to persist.

Overall, the company’s performance has been very good over the last two years. All metrics are improving and look like they will continue to improve going forward. The decent cash pile supports its ability to continue to expand through establishing more campuses in the U.S. and building out facilities in Penang.

The future looks rather bright, and the management is clearly taking advantage of the current AI craze. Investors have bid up the company’s share price significantly over the past year, which is up almost 300% as of right now, and a whopping 40% in just 5 days, which is a little concerning and suspicious. Someone must have known something about the recent news that came out this morning.

The party is not over

The recent news I am talking about is that the management filed an 8K report regarding the medium-term outlook for the company during the Needham Growth Conference in New York. The filing and the comments say that the company expects to grow its top line by between 15%-20% for the next three full years (‘25-’27) and double earnings in the same period. The news sent the company’s shares up another 20% at one point on Tuesday, and another 5% the following day, as of writing this update. Clearly, this news was very unexpected, which warranted such a reaction from the investing community.

The news is still so fresh that most of the analysts who are covering the company haven’t updated their EPS estimates, apart from Truist, who already has the company’s EPS at around $4.72 by 2027.

So, given the ridiculous demand for everything AI and data center related, the company will continue to ride these tailwinds for a lot longer, at least to 2027. It is no surprise that the hyperscalers are continuing to increase their AI-related CAPEX from already-elevated numbers that they produced for 2025. Companies like Meta (META), Amazon (AMZN), and Microsoft (MSFT) confirmed their plans to increase capex spending related to AI for 2026 to be notably higher than 2025. This is a tailwind that will help companies like TTMI and others that provide the products that enable the hyperscalers to do what they want to do with AI and data centers. The recent bottlenecks in memory products required in AI and data center applications are a prime example of demand surpassing supply massively, and currently, there may not be a supply shortage for the company’s HDI PCBs, backplanes, and RF components, but that doesn’t mean that this is going to be like that going forward. There was no shortage of memory supply very recently, and look where we are now. If the expansion efforts in Syracuse and Penang take longer than expected, I believe there will be some bottlenecks that will increase pricing for the vital parts to enable hyperscalers to continue to grow at the pace that they want. Memory stocks like Micron (MU), SanDisk (SNDK), and Western Digital (WDC), all of which received either a Buy or a Strong Buy throughout the year, have massively outperformed the market, increasing from 185% to 429% since my articles were published.

We are seeing a similar situation happen to TTMI’s share price, which is up almost 300%. It seems that investors already see that there might be some bottlenecks in the future, which will help it improve its profitability further. Does it mean the growth is already priced in? That is a tough one to answer. I am leaning more towards the idea that the super cycle is going to continue, and the company will see a massive increase in its operations, but also that will mean the company’s future earnings releases have no room for anything other than perfection.

Valuation

Let’s see if it is already priced in with a quick DCF model. Below are the assumptions I went ahead with that reflect the company’s current updated outlook.

For revenues, I went with around the midpoint of what the company said it can achieve (15%-20% top-line growth). For operating margins to give me $4.71 EPS for 2027, which is what Truist has for that year, I went with around 19.4% that increases only slightly over the next 5 years. The tax rate seemed to be too high, so I went with around 23%, and since the company is increasing its capex to meet the demand, I went ahead with a much higher number for the next few years that will stabilize to around 6% in the long run. Also, since PP&E will be higher, so will be the D&A. These assumptions give us the following EPS projections.

For my intrinsic value calculation, I decided to use 10% as my discount rate instead of 11.2% that the company’s current WACC. I find it too high, so that also means there is essentially no margin of safety. With that said, we get the company’s intrinsic value to be right where it currently trades.

If we want to be a bit more conservative and use the company’s WACC as our discount rate, we get around 14% downside from its current valuation.

Closing Comments

So, at these levels, there seems to be very little upside, if any, when looking at the projections and assumptions. It seems like everything is already priced in. Does that mean that it is? Of course not. The sentiment is on these companies’ side right now. Exuberance is through the roof, so I don’t doubt we will see further share price increases going forward. It just means to me that it is not a good time to jump in, especially because I am already trying to diversify some of my holdings away from AI plays, which will no doubt cost me some gains, but I don’t feel comfortable having all my eggs in this basket for now.

So, if you already own TTMI and enjoyed the gains so far, there is still a lot of room to run, and I think you either continue to hold it or trim some of the top and leave the rest running.

data.title: Franklin Biotechnology Discovery Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Global equities delivered solid gains in 2025’s third quarter, buoyed in part by easing trade tensions and optimism surrounding artificial intelligence (AI). Central bank actions—notably the US Federal Reserve’s policy interest- rate cut and its signaling of potential further monetary easing—supported market sentiment, as did generally robust corporate earnings. In broad terms, health care stocks exhibited improving fundamentals and posted modest gains, but they also underperformed all but two out of 10 other major equity sectors globally (per MSCI index data in US dollar terms). Among the six health care-related industries, overall gains for biotechnology stocks roughly tripled the sector average, though life sciences tools and services companies, along with other technology-enabled segments (health care-focused software, data, digital health, health technology) also fared well. In contrast to biotech’s rally, pharmaceuticals companies lagged the sector average while the health care equipment and supplies industry sold off. Market headwinds came from large pharma—pressured by patent expirations, drug-pricing risks and potential tariff measures—and from weaker results among equipment and supplies companies facing cost and demand challenges.
  • Contributors: Robust support from stock selection in the fund’s core biotechnology and pharmaceuticals holdings.
  • Detractors: Adverse stock selection and underweighting in the life sciences tools and services industry, along with selloffs for some of the fund’s higher-conviction biotech holdings.
  • Outlook: The portfolio continues to be overweighted in mid-, small- and micro-capitalization biotechnology stocks as these market-cap tiers are where we see some of the best value heading into the fourth quarter. We also continue to emphasize selectivity, favoring what we believe to be clinically or commercially “derisked” assets.

Performance Review

  • The fund’s biotech holdings collectively gained more than twice as much as those tracked by the NASDAQ Biotechnology Index. Key contributors included Abivax (ABVX), which posted a stock return of about 1,000%, along with double- or triple-digit percentage stock gains for overweighted/off-benchmark positions in uniQure, Mineralys Therapeutics (MLYS), Merus (MRUS) (sold by period-end), Centessa Pharmaceuticals (CNTA), Insmed, Olema Pharmaceuticals (OLMA) and more than two dozen others. Our biotech results were also supported by a lack of exposure to numerous poor-performing index component companies that sold off.
  • We believe the fund was benefiting from the shift toward non-acute, data analytics and remote care, while specialty pharma/digital services remained as a structural theme. Renewed investor interest in growth areas such as health tech was supported by easing macro pressures, stabilizing inflation expectations and hopes for regulatory moderation. Many biotech companies were benefiting from positive clinical trial data and groundbreaking AI developments in drug discovery, CRISPR (clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats) gene editing, mRNA (messenger ribonucleic acid) therapies, obesity/diabetes specialty drug growth, and personalized medicine.
  • Among the biotech contributors cited above, Insmed was one of our largest positions. Insmed, which specializes in rare and serious lung diseases, received US Food & Drug Administration (FDA) approval of Brinsupri to treat bronchiectasis (an inflammatory lung condition). The drug received a broad label with no unexpected safety warnings or limitations. This is the first-ever approved treatment for non-cystic fibrosis bronchiectasis.
  • The fund would have fared even better versus the Nasdaq Biotechnology Index if not for detrimental stock selection in the life sciences tools and services industry. This is where key detractor Avantor (AVTR) and other off-index holdings sold off, and where the fund’s lack of investment in several rallying index component companies—namely Medpace Holdings (MEDP) and Tempus AI (TEM)— further dampened relative performance. In the pharma industry, mild losses for off-benchmark Eli Lilly (LLY) and a slightly overweight stake in Crinetics Pharmaceuticals (CRNX) (sold by period-end) eroded the fund’s absolute and relative gains.
  • The rest of the key detractors pressured our otherwise solid results in biotech, where overweight or off-benchmark ARS Pharmaceuticals (SPRY), MoonLake Immunotherapeutics (MLTX) (sold by period-end), Soleno Therapeutics (SLNO), Akebia Therapeutics (AKBA) and Cullinan Therapeutics (CGEM) failed to advance. Underweighted positions in Alnylam Pharmaceuticals and BeOne Medicines (ONC) (formerly BeiGene) further curbed our results as they both rose about 40%, though Alnylam was still a major fund holding that added solidly to the fund’s standalone gains.

Outlook

  • Our positive outlook is based on what we consider to be generally strong fundamentals and appealing valuations backed partly by innovation in new drug modalities, what we see as a potentially strong merger-and-acquisition (M&A) cycle returning later this year and in 2026—after sticking points related to tariffs and Most Favored Nation (MFN) pricing policies are resolved—and relatively stable Medicare drug reimbursement. As it pertains to M&A, we are still waiting to see specific implementation details in terms of how the Trump administration works through looming tariff and MFN pricing policy actions. Some headway was made on both fronts in September, as select pharmaceutical firms began to forge detailed agreements (including exemptions) with the US government. We view these as important “clearing events” that can help quell investor uncertainty and potentially open up biotech M&A activity once they’re clarified.
  • Conversely, we remain cognizant of risks to biopharma innovation and stock performance linked to the persistence of inflation and the potential for only minimal interest-rate reductions in the United States for the remainder of 2025. We are also concerned about worse-than-expected impacts from the long-awaited implementation of Inflation Reduction Act (IRA, signed into law in 2022) drug pricing measures in the United States; its new prescription drug price negotiation provision, inflation caps and Medicare Part D redesign hold the potential to compress profit margins and deter innovation. With negotiated prices now public, empirical evidence is starting to test investor fears that have been active for about three years now. That said, we don’t want to overstate the risk because the IRA pertains to US revenues, and only Medicare revenues therein, which means drugs that skew towards older populations.

data.title: Franklin Intermediate Government Bond SMA Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Market sentiment improved across the third quarter of 2025 (Q3) due to a partial resolution of some of the outstanding trade policy concerns and a rate cut of 25 basis points (bps) by the US Federal Reserve (Fed), which were both well received by the market. The Fed signaled that more cuts were likely to come by the end of this year. This bolstered markets, which viewed that the cut was addressing some of the weakening economic measures, such as the status of the US labor market. Over the period, US Treasury (UST) yields moved lower with the yield curve steepening. The one-month UST bill fell 11 bps, and three-month UST bill yields declined 36 bps. Yields moved lower by 11 bps to 3.61% on the two-year UST note and by six bps to 3.74% on the 1ve-year UST note. By the end of the quarter, the yield on the benchmark 10-year UST note moved eight bps lower to 4.15%.
  • Contributors: Security selection among USTs. Overweight 10-year portion of the UST yield curve.
  • Detractors: Underweight short-duration USTs.
  • Outlook: The path of the fed funds rate is uncertain. The US job market has shown signs of stress with low job creation; however, inflation remains well above the Fed’s target. We are projecting one to two cuts in the remainder of the year. In our view, the Fed will have much room to continue to cut in an aggressive fashion in 2026 due to stubborn inflation impacted by tariff increases. In our view, intermediate- and long-maturity UST yields will move higher over the course of the fourth quarter.

Performance Review

  • Yield curve positioning detracted from returns primarily from an underweight to the two-year portion of the UST yield curve and yields fell in Q3. In contrast, overweight exposure to the 10-year area of the curve contributed to results. Security selection in UST helped returns for the period.

Outlook

  • The Fed has been struggling to balance its dual mandate—maximum employment and price stability—as stagflation (rising inflation with slowing growth) becomes a real possibility. The Fed has chosen to prioritize employment over inflation, consistent with its historical tendency to intervene when growth slows.
  • The labor market remains within the Fed’s target (unemployment at 4.3%), but inflation is still at 3% and showing little signs that it is returning to the central bank’s 2.0% target. With hiring rates low and unemployment drifting up, the Fed now sees employment risks as more pressing than inflation risks, prompting the first rate cut this year and signaling more cuts ahead.
  • US trade policy remains a wild card, adding to uncertainty in the market. Even if tariffs don’t cause a permanent lift in inflation, strong consumer spending and supportive 1scal policy mean the current economic environment is not likely to return to a disinflation level. In our view, tariffs are expected to cause a moderate, one-off burst of inflation, about 1.0-1.5 percentage points. The overall impact should be limited due to the small share of imports in the US economy, but there’s uncertainty about supply chain effects and the risk of prolonged inflation. In our view, it would be very difficult for the Fed to pivot back in the direction of holding the fed funds rate, or potentially increasing it, given the Fed’s movement toward easing financial conditions, even if inflation moves sustainably higher.
  • There is high uncertainty about future policy, even between members of the FOMC, as reflected in the large distribution seen in the Fed’s SEP scattered projections for the path of fed funds. The FOMC was nearly evenly split between those seeing two or more additional cuts by the end of this year (necessary to defend economic growth and the US job market) and those projecting just one cut (members that may have concerns over lingering inflation and tariff worries). In our view, this level of uncertainty has the potential to spur volatility in the US Treasury market.
